key_claim: Training generative models on model-generated content causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original distribution disappear.
methodology: Theoretical analysis and empirical demonstration of model collapse across Variational Autoencoders, Gaussian Mixture Models, and LLMs trained on recursively generated data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how model collapse specifically affects social-reasoning capabilities or whether provenance-aware data curation could mitigate tail-loss effects.
theoretical_framework: Model Collapse theory describing irreversible distributional tail loss in iterative generative training

key_claim: AI-generated synthetic medical data causes ~98.9% vocabulary collapse, eliminates rare findings, and triples false reassurance rates to 40% despite high diagnostic confidence.
methodology: Analysis of 800K+ synthetic medical data points to quantify vocabulary collapse, rare-finding disappearance, and false reassurance rates in clinical contexts.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether provenance-aware data collection or authenticity labeling (e.g., L_auth) could prevent clinical vocabulary collapse, nor whether inference-time scaling mitigates degradation.

key_claim: SIMS uses self-synthesized data as negative guidance during generation to avoid model collapse, enabling self-improvement without MAD degradation.
methodology: Self-IMproving diffusion models with Synthetic data (SIMS): synthetic data is used as negative guidance to steer the generative process away from the non-ideal synthetic data manifold.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether distinguishing authentic from synthetic data via provenance metadata could further mitigate collapse, nor applicability to language models or social-reasoning tasks.
theoretical_framework: Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD) framework; negative guidance via synthetic data manifold avoidance

key_claim: Introducing an idealized correction function makes self-consuming generative training loops exponentially more stable, preventing collapse.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of self-consuming loops with correction functions, plus empirical validation using self-correction functions based on expert knowledge (e.g., physics simulators).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether data authenticity provenance or L_auth-style labeling could serve as a practical correction signal, nor whether these methods apply to social-reasoning domains.
theoretical_framework: Self-correcting self-consuming loops with idealized correction functions for exponential stabilization of generative training

key_claim: Indiscriminate use of model-generated content in training causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original data distribution disappear.
methodology: Theoretical analysis and empirical demonstration of model collapse across LLMs, VAEs, and Gaussian mixture models trained on recursively generated data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether provenance-aware data curation or authenticity-based filtering can prevent model collapse during post-training.
theoretical_framework: Model collapse theory describing tail disappearance in learned distributions under recursive self-training

key_claim: Data curation by users in self-consuming generative models can provably optimize human preferences and prevent model collapse.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of iterated retraining with curated synthetic data, modeling user selection/feedback on generated outputs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether data provenance tracking (authenticity) rather than human preference curation is sufficient to maintain training quality across generations.
theoretical_framework: Theoretical framework for self-consuming generative models with curated data and human preference optimization

key_claim: Reversing gradient updates from self-training on synthetic data extrapolates away from degraded weights, turning model collapse into a self-improvement signal.
methodology: Neon method: fine-tune a base model on self-synthesized data, then reverse gradient updates to extrapolate away from degraded weights; includes theoretical proof and empirical validation on image generation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether incorporating authentic human-generated data with provenance guarantees would outperform or complement the Neon negative extrapolation approach.
theoretical_framework: Anti-alignment bias theory showing predictable degradation from high-probability-region samplers enables negative extrapolation

key_claim: Model collapse can be characterized directly from next-token probability distributions without needing to distinguish human-authored from AI-generated text.
methodology: Introduction of new measures (surplexity) derived from next-token probability distributions to characterize and mitigate model collapse under autophagy conditions.
limitation: Existing mitigation methods assume reliable knowledge of whether training data is human-authored or AI-generated, which is often unavailable.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether provenance-aware data collection (e.g., L_auth or CampusGo-style authenticity tracking) could complement distribution-based collapse detection.
theoretical_framework: Surplexity: a framework for measuring model collapse via next-token probability distribution analysis

key_claim: Up to 22.5% of computer science papers showed LLM modification by September 2024, demonstrating large-scale AI contamination of scientific literature.
methodology: Population-level analysis of 1.1 million scientific papers to quantify LLM-modified content prevalence across disciplines.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether LLM-modified scientific text, when recycled into training corpora, degrades downstream social-reasoning capabilities of models.

key_claim: 74.2% of new English webpages contain some AI content, but only 2.5% are purely AI-generated.
methodology: Large-scale analysis of 900K English webpages crawled in April 2025 with AI content detection to estimate prevalence of AI-generated and AI-assisted content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the distinction between 'any AI' and 'pure AI' content matters for provenance-aware data collection and post-training data authenticity effects on model reasoning.

key_claim: AI-generated articles surpassed human-written articles in raw volume around November 2024 at ~52%, but 86% of Google-ranking articles remain human-written.
methodology: Analysis of 65K URLs sampled from Common Crawl to estimate the proportion of AI-generated vs human-written articles over time.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether quality-filtered corpora (e.g., FineWeb, RefinedWeb) successfully preserve data authenticity and whether provenance-aware filtering mitigates model collapse in social reasoning.

key_claim: 67% pool contamination with AI-generated content leads to over 80% exposure contamination in retrieval systems, creating homogenized yet deceptively healthy search results.
methodology: Controlled experiments simulating SEO-style and adversarial AI content contamination in retrieval pipelines, comparing BM25 and LLM-based rankers under varying contamination levels.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether retrieval collapse affects downstream post-training data authenticity when models use RAG-retrieved content, and whether provenance-aware collection (e.g., CampusGo) can mitigate this feedback loop.
theoretical_framework: Retrieval Collapse: a two-stage ecosystem-level failure mode where AI-generated content dominates search results and low-quality or adversarial content infiltrates retrieval pipelines

key_claim: AI-generated content is highly prevalent in MOOC student responses and can be identified through textual analysis metrics at scale.
methodology: Large-scale textual analysis of over 4045 student responses from an introductory IoT MOOC using various metrics to distinguish AI-generated from human-generated text.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether data authenticity or provenance-aware curation of training corpora influences the social-reasoning capabilities of the LLMs generating these responses.
dataset_or_benchmark: 4045 MOOC student responses from an IoT course

key_claim: AI-generated web content causes Retrieval Collapse, where 67% pool contamination leads to over 80% exposure contamination in search results while masking accuracy degradation.
methodology: Controlled experiments simulating SEO-style and adversarial AI-generated content contamination of retrieval pipelines, comparing BM25 baselines with LLM-based rankers.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the authenticity or provenance of post-training data used by LLMs affects their social-reasoning quality or downstream behavioral outcomes.
dataset_or_benchmark: controlled web retrieval experiments (SEO and adversarial scenarios)

key_claim: AI-generated hyperrealistic wildlife images and videos threaten conservation by distorting public perceptions of species diversity and animal behavior.
methodology: Qualitative analysis identifying three societal characteristics (social media use, anthropomorphism, nature disconnection) that exacerbate risks of AI-generated wildlife media.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance-aware data curation or training data authenticity could mitigate the generation of misleading AI wildlife content.

key_claim: A fine-tuned Turkish BERT classifier achieves 0.9708 F1 score for detecting AI-rewritten content in Turkish news media with temporally stable classification patterns.
methodology: Fine-tuning dbmdz/bert-base-turkish-cased on 3,600 labeled articles from three Turkish outlets for binary AI-rewritten content classification, then deploying on 3,500+ unseen articles (2023–2026).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the authenticity or provenance of the LLMs' training data influences the social-reasoning or factual quality of the AI-generated news content being detected.

key_claim: A supervised detection framework can estimate the prevalence of unmarked AI-generated content in leading Russian online news media.
methodology: Supervised binary text classification applied to a large corpus of news texts from 20 top Russian online media outlets collected March–May 2025, selected via SimilarWeb.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data provenance or authenticity of training corpora affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities of LLMs.

key_claim: The Dead Internet Theory is substantiated by the growing prevalence of bots and AI-generated content that fundamentally alter the structure of online interactions.
methodology: Critical literature review and investigation of AI-driven content generation models (e.g., GPT-3) and bot activity reports (e.g., Imperva) to assess the Dead Internet Theory.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how synthetic content contamination of training data affects LLM social-reasoning performance or post-training data authenticity.

key_claim: Approximately 12% of images shared on Twitter/X during the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election are AI-generated, with 10% of users responsible for 80% of their dissemination.
methodology: Large-scale analysis of Twitter/X images using AI-generated image detection, characterizing superspreaders by account features, political leaning, and engagement metrics.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the prevalence of AI-generated political content in training corpora affects LLM social reasoning or post-training data authenticity.

key_claim: Synthetic data contamination in training datasets degrades online continual learning performance, which can be mitigated by entropy-based selection of real vs. synthetic samples.
methodology: Experimental evaluation of online continual learning methods on datasets contaminated with diffusion-generated synthetic images; proposed Entropy Selection with Real-synthetic discrimination.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how synthetic contamination affects LLM post-training for social reasoning or whether provenance-aware data collection (e.g., CampusGo) could mitigate such contamination.

key_claim: AI-generated labels on social media posts show stable cognitive effects over 18 days but induce a small negative affective shift ('emotional tax') when applied to true content.
methodology: Two-wave online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) manipulating veracity and content type of labeled health posts, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the provenance or authenticity of the training data behind AI-generated content influences users' social reasoning or trust calibration.

key_claim: AI-generated labels on social media posts show stable cognitive effects over 18 days but induce a small negative affective shift ('emotional tax') when applied to true content.
methodology: Two-wave online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) manipulating veracity and content type of labeled health posts, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the provenance or authenticity of post-training data shapes the social-reasoning capabilities of the AI systems generating the content.

key_claim: A preregistered experiment across 27 European countries examines how people perceive and intend to share AI-generated versus human-generated fake news about the Russo-Ukrainian war.
methodology: Large-scale preregistered web survey experiment across 27 European countries; participants assessed perceived veracity and sharing intentions for eight news headlines (AI- vs. human-generated, real vs. fake).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the training data authenticity or provenance of the generative AI models affects the persuasiveness or social-reasoning outcomes of the generated misinformation.

key_claim: BERT-based models (ALBERT, IndicBERT) and SVM effectively classify AI-generated versus human-written product reviews in Malayalam and Tamil.
methodology: Classification using ALBERT, IndicBERT, and SVM models on a shared task dataset of product reviews in Dravidian languages (Malayalam and Tamil).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how training data provenance or authenticity (L_auth) affects model social-reasoning capabilities or whether inference-time scaling could improve detection.

key_claim: The arrival of AI-generated content on Pixiv has measurably impacted the social media ecosystem including community dynamics and content engagement patterns.
methodology: Large-scale quantitative analysis of 15.2 million posts on Pixiv, comparing human-created and AI-generated content across social engagement metrics.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance-aware data collection or authenticity labeling of AI-generated content affects downstream model social-reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: A watermark embedded via green-token promotion during LLM sampling is statistically detectable from short text spans with negligible impact on text quality.
methodology: Watermarking framework that selects randomized 'green' token sets before generation and softly promotes them during sampling; detection via statistical hypothesis testing with p-values; information-theoretic sensitivity analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarking interacts with post-training data authenticity or whether watermarked provenance metadata could improve social-reasoning outcomes in downstream models.
theoretical_framework: Information-theoretic framework for watermark sensitivity analysis

key_claim: Paraphrasing AI-generated text with an 11B parameter model (DIPPER) evades multiple detectors, but retrieval-based defense can restore robustness.
methodology: Built DIPPER, an 11B parameter paraphrase generation model controlling lexical diversity and content reordering; stress-tested watermarking, GPTZero, DetectGPT, and OpenAI's classifier; proposed retrieval-based defense.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether paraphrased AI-generated text entering training corpora undermines data authenticity and downstream social-reasoning performance.

key_claim: Targeted masking perturbation on high-perplexity code regions paired with fine-tuned CodeBERT infilling can effectively detect AI-generated code assignments.
methodology: Targeted masking perturbation focusing on high-perplexity areas of code, followed by CodeBERT-based infilling and comprehensive scoring to distinguish AI-generated from human-written code.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how undetected AI-generated code in training data affects data authenticity or model reasoning capabilities in social or non-code domains.

key_claim: Hybrid multi-modal detection approaches achieve up to 92% accuracy for synthetic content, outperforming single-modality methods like stylometric analysis at 80%.
methodology: Systematic review of detection approaches across modalities including stylometric analysis, watermarking, pixel prediction, dual-stream networks, ML models, blockchain, and hybrid methods
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the provenance or authenticity of post-training data affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities of generative models

key_claim: Detectability of AI-generated text depends on a combination of salient factors including generation method, domain, and detection technique employed.
methodology: Survey and synthesis of state-of-the-art AIGT detection approaches including watermarking, statistical and stylistic analysis, and ML classification, with review of existing datasets
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how data authenticity or provenance of training/post-training corpora influences model social-reasoning behavior

key_claim: Recursively training language models on synthetic text produced by predecessors leads to consistent decline in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity across generations.
methodology: Recursive finetuning experiments across multiple NLG tasks in English, evaluated with novel and adapted metrics for lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the provenance-authenticated (human-sourced) post-training data could mitigate diversity collapse or preserve social-reasoning capabilities

key_claim: The proposed (α-Precision, β-Recall, Authenticity) 3D metric enables sample-level evaluation of generative models, where Authenticity quantifies whether generated samples are novel versus copied from training data.
methodology: Formal definition and operationalization of three complementary sample-level metrics (α-Precision, β-Recall, Authenticity) for auditing generative model outputs
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how training data provenance (as in L_auth) predicts downstream task capability such as social reasoning; its authenticity concept concerns sample-level fidelity/novelty rather than data-source provenance effects
theoretical_framework: α-Precision, β-Recall, Authenticity framework for generative model evaluation

key_claim: Bangla literary corpora exhibit significantly higher lexical richness and structural variation than newspaper corpora despite smaller size.
methodology: Corpus-driven statistical analysis using type-token ratio, hapax legomena ratio, bigram diversity, syllable/word lengths, Zipf's Law adherence, and n-gram perplexity on Bangla corpora.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how corpus-level lexical diversity or provenance differences affect downstream social-reasoning capabilities of language models.

key_claim: There exists an empirical functional relation between word entropy and type-token ratio in large corpora, analytically derivable from Zipf's and Heaps' laws.
methodology: Quantitative analysis of entropy and type-token ratio across six gigaword corpora in three languages, deriving an analytical expression in the large-text-length limit using Zipf's and Heaps' laws.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how differences in lexical diversity across corpus sources affect model behavior on social reasoning or post-training data selection.
dataset_or_benchmark: gigaword corpora of books, news articles, and tweets in English, Spanish, and Turkish

key_claim: There exists an empirical functional relation between word entropy and type-token ratio in large corpora, analytically derivable from Zipf's and Heaps' laws.
methodology: Quantitative analysis of entropy and type-token ratio across six gigaword corpora in three languages, with an analytical derivation for the large-text-length limit.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not explore how corpus provenance or lexical diversity metrics relate to data authenticity considerations for post-training of language models.
dataset_or_benchmark: gigaword corpora of books, news articles, and tweets in English, Spanish, and Turkish

key_claim: Ratio-matching via denoising cross-entropy outperforms score-entropy in discrete diffusion language models with up to 10% lower perplexity and 15% faster training.
methodology: Introduces three new KL-divergence theorems for discrete diffusion (CTMC framework), derives an improved perplexity upper bound, and empirically compares denoising cross-entropy vs. score-entropy ratio-matching.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how data provenance or authenticity of training corpora affects discrete diffusion model performance on social-reasoning tasks.
theoretical_framework: Continuous-time discrete Markov chain (CTMC) framework for discrete diffusion

key_claim: An NLP-based approach can distinguish AI-generated phishing messages from both legitimate emails and human-written phishing attempts.
methodology: Assembled a balanced dataset from authentic emails, human-written phishing samples, and synthetic AI-generated phishing messages; applied NLP-based classification techniques.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether data provenance or authenticity metadata of training data influences the social-reasoning capabilities of the generative models producing phishing content.
dataset_or_benchmark: custom dataset of authentic emails, human-written phishing, and AI-generated phishing

key_claim: Traditional corpus-based complexity indices (TTR, word-level Entropy) are less robust to corpus size and content variation than newer indices (Word Information Density, Lexical Diversity) across 47 languages.
methodology: Systematic robustness testing of four complexity indices (TTR, word-level Entropy, Word Information Density, Lexical Diversity) estimated from raw texts across 47 languages under varying corpus sizes and content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how post-training data authenticity or provenance affects language model performance on social reasoning or complexity estimation tasks.
theoretical_framework: Cross-linguistic trade-off between word-internal and across-word distributional complexity

key_claim: An adaptive watermarking strategy that selectively watermarks high-entropy tokens and scales logits based on semantic embeddings improves text quality, security, and robustness over fixed watermarking methods.
methodology: Adaptive watermark insertion on high-entropy token distributions measured via an auxiliary model; logit scaling proportional to semantic embeddings of previous context instead of fixed green/red lists.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarking interacts with post-training data authenticity or provenance-aware data collection for social-reasoning tasks.

key_claim: Recursive self-training acts as a sharpening operator on data distributions, causing systematic tail collapse and loss of structural diversity, which is the central mechanism of model degradation.
methodology: Formalization of recursive self-training as an iterative distributional transformation; controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance-aware data collection (e.g., CampusGo) or data authenticity labeling can mitigate the distributional collapse described, nor how these dynamics specifically affect social-reasoning outcomes.
theoretical_framework: Loss Distribution Collapse framework with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Recursive self-training collapses the loss distribution by sharpening it, systematically eliminating tail samples, and stable learning requires preserving tail probability mass across generations.
methodology: Formal iterative distributional transformation analysis combined with controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance-aware data collection (e.g., authentic vs. synthetic sourcing) can mitigate tail collapse, nor whether inference-time scaling compensates for lost distributional diversity.
theoretical_framework: Loss Distribution Collapse theory with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Lexical diversity in Czech L2 texts increases with proficiency level, with significant differences between Slavic and non-Slavic native speaker groups.
methodology: Corpus-based quantitative analysis using Moving Average Type-Token Ratio (MATTR) across CEFR proficiency levels A1–C1.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data authenticity or provenance of learner texts affects downstream NLP model training or social-reasoning evaluations.

key_claim: CurveMark combines probability curvature analysis with dynamic semantic watermarking to detect AI-generated text without requiring prior knowledge of the source model.
methodology: Dual-channel detection framework using information-theoretic probability curvature features and entropy-aware semantic watermark embedding, with Bayesian multi-hypothesis inference.
limitation: Current detection methods suffer from limited information capture, poor rate-distortion trade-offs, and vulnerability to adversarial perturbations (motivating limitation rather than self-acknowledged limitation of CurveMark itself); not_stated for CurveMark specifically.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how provenance metadata or authentic data collection pipelines could complement or replace post-hoc AI-text detection for ensuring training data authenticity.
theoretical_framework: Information-theoretic mutual information maximization with Bayesian multi-hypothesis detection

key_claim: Source language properties systematically shape what English models learn from machine-translated data: lexical diversity drives perplexity while source-target syntactic similarity drives grammatical performance.
methodology: Training small English language models on machine-translated text from 24 typologically diverse source languages, then evaluating linguistic acceptability judgments and language modeling across domains.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether using authentic (non-translated) data with verified provenance would yield different social-reasoning outcomes compared to translated training data, nor how inference-time scaling interacts with translationese effects.

key_claim: Integral probability metrics with bounded test classes can be evaluated from finite samples up to multiplicative and additive approximation errors.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of evaluability for generative model metrics, studying IPMs and Rényi divergences under finite-sample regimes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data provenance or authenticity of training data affects the reliability of generative model evaluation metrics.
theoretical_framework: Statistical evaluability framework for generative models based on IPMs and Rényi divergences

key_claim: LLM-prompted authorship impersonation texts fail to evade existing forensic authorship verification methods.
methodology: Generated impersonation texts using GPT-4o under four prompting conditions across three genres, evaluated against non-neural (n-gram tracing, RBI, LambdaG) and neural (AdHominem, LUAR, STAR) AV methods within a likelihood-ratio framework.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether post-training data authenticity or provenance-aware fine-tuning would alter LLMs' ability to produce more convincing impersonations.

key_claim: Augmenting reverse KL with forward KL under high teacher entropy maintains generation diversity and improves student-teacher alignment in on-policy distillation.
methodology: Entropy-aware on-policy distillation that dynamically augments reverse KL with forward KL based on teacher entropy, evaluated on language model distillation tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the authenticity or provenance of the training data used by the teacher model affects the quality of distilled social-reasoning capabilities.
theoretical_framework: Entropy-aware combination of forward and reverse KL divergence objectives

key_claim: Projecting intermediate hidden states into vocabulary space via Logit Lens and enforcing symmetric divergence alignment consistently outperforms standard knowledge distillation.
methodology: DistillLens framework using Logit Lens to project intermediate hidden states into vocabulary space, applying symmetric divergence objectives for structural alignment between teacher and student models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether provenance or authenticity of post-training data affects the fidelity of intermediate-layer thought process transfer during distillation.
theoretical_framework: Symmetric divergence-based intermediate representation alignment via Logit Lens

key_claim: Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) mitigates mode collapse in RLVR by equilibrating confidence across correct responses, redistributing probability mass toward under-explored reasoning paths.
methodology: Proposes ARM that incorporates Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into advantage estimation to dynamically reshape reward signals during GRPO-based policy optimization.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether post-training data authenticity or provenance influences the diversity or quality of reasoning chains produced under RLVR.
theoretical_framework: Sampling probability dynamics analysis of GRPO objective; Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM)

key_claim: ForgetMark achieves 100% ownership verification on fingerprinted language models by encoding provenance via targeted unlearning rather than fixed trigger-response backdoors.
methodology: Builds human-readable key-value sets using an assistant model and predictive-entropy ranking, trains lightweight LoRA adapters to suppress original values, and verifies ownership via aggregated likelihood and semantic evidence.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how training data authenticity or provenance-aware data collection affects the social-reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
theoretical_framework: Probabilistic forgetting traces framework for model fingerprinting

key_claim: The minimum of Renyi entropy coincides with the true number of topics in labelled collections, enabling principled analysis of regularization effects on topic models.
methodology: Applies Renyi entropy analysis inspired by statistical physics to evaluate four topic models (pLSA, BigARTM, LDA with Gibbs sampling, VLDA) and compares regularization effects on topical structure.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how topic modeling or entropy-based analysis relates to post-training data authenticity or social-reasoning outcomes in LLMs.
theoretical_framework: Renyi entropy framework from statistical physics applied to information-theoretic analysis of topic models

key_claim: Topic model outputs exhibit self-similar behavior under variation of cluster number, enabling renormalization techniques combined with Renyi entropy for quick optimal topic number search.
methodology: Develops a renormalization procedure for pLSA, VLDA, and GLDA topic models, combining it with Renyi entropy to optimize the number of topics without exhaustive grid search.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address connections between topic model optimization and data provenance or authenticity in the context of LLM post-training.
theoretical_framework: Renormalization group technique from statistical physics combined with Renyi entropy

key_claim: A two-stage deduplication and model-based quality scoring pipeline yields a 35TB bilingual pretraining dataset with 4.5 billion diverse Chain-of-Thought templates.
methodology: Two-stage deduplication, multi-classifier quality scoring, domain-aware fluency filtering, and staged CoT extraction from diverse sources rather than distillation from larger models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how data provenance or authenticity of the source web documents affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: A lightweight multitask model trained on LLM-annotated Romanian texts enables multi-level filtering that improves LLM pretraining performance across multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Training a lightweight multitask classifier on LLM-annotated Romanian texts to perform multi-level filtering (educational value, topic, format) of pretraining corpora.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether provenance or authenticity of the source data influences social-reasoning or cultural understanding in under-represented languages.

key_claim: Yi models' strong performance across benchmarks is primarily attributable to data quality achieved through cascaded deduplication and quality filtering of 3.1 trillion tokens.
methodology: Classical transformer architecture with cascaded data deduplication and quality filtering pipeline for pretraining on 3.1T English and Chinese tokens; iterative polishing of a small (<10K) instruction-tuning dataset for finetuning.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether post-training data authenticity or provenance tracking affects social-reasoning outcomes.

key_claim: Classifier-based quality filtering improves downstream task performance not by capturing meaningful data quality but by implicitly filtering the high-quality reference set itself.
methodology: In-depth analysis of classifier-based quality filtering (CQF); comparison of CQF-trained models against models trained on synthetic data with controlled quality degradation via random token permutations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether provenance-aware data collection or authenticity verification could provide a more meaningful quality signal than classifier-based scoring.

key_claim: Grounded synthetic data generation at 540B tokens across 10 Indic languages can substantially improve low-resource language pretraining quality.
methodology: Systematic generation of synthetic multilingual pretraining data using 5 techniques (document-grounded, persona-based, topic-based generation, translation, and native generation) with a modular quality evaluation pipeline.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the provenance or authenticity of seed documents used for grounding affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: Conclusions about data recipe quality from small proxy models can flip with minor hyperparameter changes because optimal training configurations are data-dependent.
methodology: Systematic comparison of data recipe assessments under fixed vs. varied hyperparameter configurations across small-scale proxy model training runs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether data provenance or authenticity properties are preserved or distorted when transferring conclusions from proxy to full-scale models.

key_claim: Improving HTML-to-text extraction quality via a model-based parser can be as impactful as aggressive filtering for downstream LLM performance.
methodology: MinerU-HTML pipeline reformulates HTML content extraction as a sequence labeling problem using a 0.6B-parameter language model with a two-stage formatting pipeline that categorizes semantic elements before Markdown conversion.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how extraction fidelity affects the authenticity or provenance metadata of training data, nor its impact on social-reasoning benchmarks.

key_claim: Jointly optimizing data quality and diversity via a unified parameterized sampling function yields more efficient LLM pretraining than sequential quality filtering and proportion adjustment.
methodology: QuaDMix framework using multiple quality criteria and domain classification for diversity measurement, combined with a parameterized data sampling function that automatically optimizes data distribution for pretraining.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider data authenticity or provenance as a selection criterion, nor evaluate effects on social-reasoning tasks.

key_claim: Sample-wise cross-domain data mixing that jointly evaluates quality and diversity of each sample surpasses existing domain-wise pre-training data mixing methods.
methodology: Bottom-up sample-wise data mixing approach that globally scores each sample's quality and diversity across domains, dynamically determining optimal domain distribution.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the provenance or authenticity of training samples (e.g., human-authored vs. synthetic) affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: RedPajama-V1 and RedPajama-V2 provide fully open, large-scale pre-training datasets with quality signals and metadata to advance transparent language model development.
methodology: Open reproduction of the LLaMA training dataset (RedPajama-V1) and release of a massive web-only dataset with raw text plus quality signals and metadata (RedPajama-V2).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data provenance or authenticity of included sources affects social-reasoning outcomes in downstream models.

key_claim: A harmless yet non-evasive AI assistant can be trained using only AI-generated feedback guided by a constitution of principles, without human labels for harmful outputs.
methodology: Constitutional AI: supervised self-critique and revision phase followed by reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) using a preference model trained on AI-generated comparisons.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether replacing human feedback with AI feedback degrades the authenticity of training signal or affects nuanced social-reasoning performance.

key_claim: Fine-tuning language models on empathetic conversation data increases safety-task failure rates by 10-30 percentage points and sycophancy by approximately 40%.
methodology: Fine-tuning on empathetic (synthetic) conversation data followed by evaluation on safety benchmarks and sycophancy metrics to quantify reliability degradation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not test whether using authentic (human-generated, provenance-verified) empathy data rather than synthetic empathy data would mitigate the observed reliability trade-offs.

key_claim: LLMs can self-align with human values using principle-driven reasoning and minimal human supervision, reducing dependence on costly human annotations.
methodology: SELF-ALIGN: a four-stage approach using LLM-generated synthetic prompts with topic-guided diversity augmentation, guided by a small set of human-written principles for self-alignment.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the synthetic training data produced by self-alignment preserves provenance authenticity or introduces distributional biases compared to human-authored data.

key_claim: BeaverTails provides separately annotated helpfulness and harmlessness labels for over 333K QA pairs, enabling improved safety alignment research for LLMs.
methodology: Human annotation of safety meta-labels and expert comparison data for QA pairs, with separate labeling of helpfulness and harmlessness; demonstrated in content moderation and RLHF applications.
what_it_does_NOT_address: How the provenance and authenticity of the annotated preference data affect downstream social-reasoning capabilities of aligned models.

key_claim: RRHF aligns language models with human preferences using ranking loss on log-conditional probabilities, achieving comparable performance to PPO with fewer models and simpler training.
methodology: RRHF scores sampled responses from multiple sources via log conditional probabilities and applies a ranking loss to align model outputs with human preferences, requiring only 1-2 models during training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the quality and authenticity of the response sources used for ranking affect social-reasoning alignment outcomes.
theoretical_framework: RRHF (Rank Responses to align with Human Feedback)

key_claim: An instructable reward model trained on synthetic preference data and guided by adjustable human-defined principles can align LLMs with minimal human supervision and achieve superior performance.
methodology: SALMON: trains an instructable reward model on synthetic preference data that generates reward scores based on arbitrary human-defined principles, used during RL training to control alignment preferences.
limitation: Dependency on the quality and coverage of the small set of human-defined principles; challenges remain in obtaining consistent annotations for intricate tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether synthetic preference data introduces provenance-related biases that degrade social-reasoning fidelity compared to authentic human-generated preference data.

key_claim: Reinforcement learning is fundamentally a human-in-the-loop paradigm and human-centric approaches are key to successful RL.
methodology: Systematic survey and position paper reviewing human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning literature, identifying requirements, challenges, and opportunities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the authenticity or provenance of human feedback data affects the quality of social reasoning in trained agents.
theoretical_framework: Human-in-the-Loop RL framework positioning human-centric design as central to RL

key_claim: RLHF can be solved with a simple classification loss by reparameterizing the reward model to extract the optimal policy in closed form.
methodology: Derives a new parameterization of the reward model enabling closed-form optimal policy extraction, replacing RL fine-tuning with a binary classification (direct preference optimization) loss.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the provenance or authenticity of preference data affects alignment quality or social reasoning outcomes.
theoretical_framework: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) — closed-form reparameterization of the RLHF reward objective

key_claim: RLAIF achieves comparable performance to RLHF across summarization and dialogue tasks, and direct-RLAIF outperforms canonical RLAIF by obtaining rewards directly from an LLM.
methodology: Compares RLHF and RLAIF by training reward models on AI-generated preference labels and evaluating on summarization, helpful dialogue, and harmless dialogue; introduces direct-RLAIF bypassing RM training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the authenticity or provenance of human vs. AI feedback data influences social-reasoning capabilities of aligned models.
theoretical_framework: RLAIF and direct-RLAIF frameworks for substituting human preference labels with AI-generated labels

key_claim: RLHF is a broadly applicable technique across multiple domains that aligns intelligent systems with human values, as demonstrated by its decisive role in LLM training.
methodology: Comprehensive literature survey covering fundamentals of RLHF, preference-based RL, and human-computer interaction across multiple domains beyond LLMs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the authenticity or provenance of post-training feedback data impacts social-reasoning quality in aligned models.
theoretical_framework: Preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) as foundational framework for RLHF

key_claim: AI assistants finetuned with human feedback consistently exhibit sycophancy, partly because humans and preference models prefer sycophantic responses over correct ones.
methodology: Evaluated five state-of-the-art AI assistants on four free-form text-generation tasks for sycophantic behavior; analyzed existing human preference data and preference model optimization.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether data authenticity or provenance-aware curation of post-training feedback could mitigate sycophancy in preference-tuned models.
theoretical_framework: Cognitive and computational framework linking human feedback, sycophancy, and degradation of the self-concept

key_claim: Distilled direct preference optimization using AI feedback enables a 7B model (Zephyr-7B) to surpass Llama2-Chat-70B on MT-Bench without human annotation.
methodology: Distilled supervised fine-tuning (dSFT) followed by distilled direct preference optimization (dDPO) using AI-generated preference rankings from a teacher model.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether replacing synthetic AI feedback with authentic human-sourced preference data would further improve alignment quality or social-reasoning outcomes.

key_claim: Distribution-guided data filtering using differential distribution rewards enables efficient LLM alignment with less data while mitigating loss of generalization.
methodology: DEFT framework that computes differential distribution rewards from output distributions and preference data discrepancy distributions to filter a high-quality data subset for fine-tuning.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the provenance or authenticity of the underlying preference data affects the quality of the filtered subset and downstream social-reasoning performance.

key_claim: Personalized federated DPO with shared LoRA adapters and client-specific LLM heads addresses non-IID heterogeneity for privacy-preserving LLM preference alignment.
methodology: FedPDPO framework combining frozen pretrained LLM backbones with LoRA adapters, globally shared adapters, personalized client-specific heads, and personalized implicit reward mechanisms under federated learning.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether authenticity and provenance of decentralized preference data across clients influences the quality of federated alignment outcomes.

key_claim: Current language models exhibit substantial misalignment with US demographic groups' opinions, on par with the Democrat-Republican divide on climate change, even after demographic steering.
methodology: Quantitative framework leveraging high-quality public opinion polls to measure alignment between LM-generated opinions and human survey responses across 60 US demographic groups.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether using more authentic or provenance-tracked training data could reduce the observed opinion misalignment across demographic groups.
theoretical_framework: Quantitative opinion-alignment framework based on public opinion polls

key_claim: LLM-simulated human feedback is 50x cheaper than crowdworkers while maintaining high agreement with humans, enabling low-cost research on learning from feedback.
methodology: Simulation framework (AlpacaFarm) using LLM prompts to approximate human feedback, with automatic evaluation validated against real-world human interactions and reference implementations of PPO, DPO, best-of-n, and expert iteration.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the authenticity and provenance of the underlying training data affect the quality of simulated feedback or downstream social reasoning.

key_claim: Process supervision significantly outperforms outcome supervision for training models to solve math problems, achieving 78% accuracy on a representative MATH test subset.
methodology: Comparison of process supervision (per-step feedback) versus outcome supervision (final-result feedback) for training reward models, combined with active learning to improve data efficiency.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the provenance or authenticity of human feedback data used for process supervision affects the reliability of social or non-mathematical reasoning.

key_claim: Mobile phone sensor data can be used to sense and model complex social systems in naturalistic settings.
methodology: Collection and analysis of behavioral data from mobile phone sensors (Bluetooth, cell tower logs, app usage) to infer social network structure and daily activity patterns.
what_it_does_NOT_address: How provenance-aware data collection from ubiquitous sensing could inform post-training data authenticity for language models' social reasoning.

key_claim: Smartphone sensing can continuously assess college students' mental health, academic performance, and behavioral trends in naturalistic settings.
methodology: Longitudinal smartphone sensing study collecting behavioral data (sleep, activity, sociability) from college students and correlating with mental health and academic outcomes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address data authenticity or provenance of the collected sensing data, nor whether such data could serve as authenticated training corpora for social-reasoning models.

key_claim: Pre-GPT human-generated data functions like 'low-background steel,' appreciating in strategic value as web-scale AI contamination increases.
methodology: Legal-strategic analysis drawing on EU competition law, essential facilities doctrine, data trustee models, and mandatory AI content labeling frameworks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically quantify how model collapse from contaminated data affects social-reasoning performance or propose a formal metric like L_auth to measure authenticity effects.
theoretical_framework: Low-background steel analogy; essential facilities doctrine applied to pre-2022 uncontaminated human-generated data

key_claim: Data authenticity verification, consent tracking, and provenance preservation are systemically broken in current AI training practices, with ~5% of C4 tokens and 45% of content restricted by terms of service.
methodology: Systematic audit of data practices across AI training pipelines; empirical analysis of consent and terms-of-service compliance in large corpora; proposal of unified data provenance standards.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not propose a quantitative framework (e.g., L_auth) measuring how provenance-aware data collection causally improves downstream social-reasoning outcomes.

key_claim: A blockchain-enabled data provenance framework achieves 95 percent reduction in provenance disputes and full traceability across AI training pipelines.
methodology: Design and evaluation of a blockchain-based architecture using immutable smart contract transactions with lightweight off-chain storage, tested across three ML pipelines (image classification, text processing, sensor analytics).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether blockchain-verified provenance improves the authenticity of post-training data in ways that affect social-reasoning model performance, nor does it engage with L_auth or inference-time scaling alternatives.
theoretical_framework: Blockchain-enabled data provenance framework with smart-contract-based compliance automation

key_claim: Effective data stewardship and curation practices are essential to maintain integrity, privacy, and accessibility of vast AI-generated genomics and HTS datasets.
methodology: Systematic literature search across PubMed, MEDLINE, EMBASE, Scopus, and other scholarly platforms up to January 2024
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data provenance or authenticity of training data affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities of language models.

key_claim: Citizen science projects perform well on data quality and governance but lack open data access, documentation, interoperability, and long-term preservation.
methodology: Qualitative semi-structured interviews with 36 citizen science project proponents using purposive landscape sampling, analyzed with descriptive statistics
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how data authenticity or provenance tracking in citizen-collected datasets influences downstream AI model training or social-reasoning outcomes.

key_claim: Stewarding volunteer-generated citizen science data involves ethical decisions beyond technical ones, driven by power asymmetries between professionals and volunteers.
methodology: Perspective/conceptual analysis examining ethical dimensions of open data governance in citizen science
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how volunteer data provenance affects the authenticity of training corpora used for AI models or their social-reasoning performance.

key_claim: Data harmonization for individual participant data pooling entails trade-offs between standardization and information loss that FAIR principles alone do not fully resolve.
methodology: Conceptual discussion of FAIR data governance principles applied to individual participant data harmonization for biomedical meta-analyses
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how provenance-aware data collection or authenticity-based curation of training data affects AI model reasoning capabilities.
theoretical_framework: FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data governance principles

key_claim: An open-access platform with version control and adaptation management can resolve fragmented provenance and governance of mental health outcome measures.
methodology: Technical specification proposing version control, provenance documentation, and community-driven governance for mental health measurement tools.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how provenance-aware data collection practices for training AI models (e.g., social reasoning) might benefit from similar versioning frameworks.

key_claim: The CARE principles provide an intellectual framework for Indigenous data governance that highlights hidden costs of data sharing borne by Indigenous communities in archaeology.
methodology: Conceptual analysis applying the CARE (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics) principles to archaeological data reuse and sharing practices.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how Indigenous data governance frameworks intersect with provenance-aware data collection for training large language models or social-reasoning AI systems.
theoretical_framework: CARE Principles (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics)

key_claim: Data integrity of NHS Digital datasets can be demonstrated by documenting data lifecycles and assessing accuracy, reliability, and completeness for clinical trial use.
methodology: Mapping data lifecycles from originating healthcare provider databases through NHS Digital warehouses to clinical trial teams, assessing provenance, quality, and reliability.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance and quality documentation methods for healthcare data could generalize to post-training data authenticity verification for language models.

key_claim: Properly filtered and deduplicated web data alone can train language models that significantly outperform models trained on curated corpora like The Pile.
methodology: Extensive filtering and deduplication pipeline applied to CommonCrawl to produce the RefinedWeb dataset; training 1.3B and 7.5B parameter language models and benchmarking against state-of-the-art models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether the provenance or authenticity of the web data sources affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities, nor whether inference-time scaling could compensate for data quality differences.

key_claim: FineWeb, a 15-trillion token dataset from 96 Common Crawl snapshots, produces better-performing LLMs than other open pretraining datasets, especially when filtered for educational content.
methodology: Systematic curation of Common Crawl data with documented deduplication and filtering strategies, ablation studies on design choices, and creation of FineWeb-Edu via educational text filtering.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how provenance or authenticity of the source web content affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities of models trained on the data.

key_claim: C4 contains significant unexpected content including machine-generated text and benchmark evaluation data, and its blocklist filtering disproportionately removes text from certain communities.
methodology: Documentation and audit of the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4) by analyzing data provenance, content composition, and the differential impact of applied filters on various text populations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the presence of machine-generated or non-authentic text in C4 affects social-reasoning or value-alignment outcomes in downstream models.

key_claim: Over 5% of newly created English Wikipedia articles are flagged as AI-generated, and these articles are typically of lower quality and more self-promotional.
methodology: Application of two AI-content detectors (GPTZero and Binoculars) with thresholds calibrated at 1% false positive rate on pre-GPT-3.5 articles to estimate lower bounds of AI-generated content in recent Wikipedia pages across multiple languages.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the infiltration of AI-generated content into Wikipedia affects the authenticity and social-reasoning quality of language models trained on web-scraped data including Wikipedia.

key_claim: A curated longitudinal dataset of over one million English privacy policies spanning two decades reveals how the privacy policy landscape changed in response to evolving regulations like GDPR.
methodology: Development of a crawler to discover, download, and extract archived privacy policies from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine, combined with NLP-based curation and longitudinal analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the provenance or authenticity of policy text in web corpora affects language model training for social reasoning or compliance understanding.

key_claim: In 2023-2024, web sources rapidly increased data restrictions, rendering over 5% of tokens in major AI corpora like C4 non-consentable for AI training.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal audit of consent protocols (robots.txt and Terms of Service) across 14,000 web domains underlying AI training corpora.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the decline of the AI data commons affects the authenticity or provenance quality of remaining training data and downstream social-reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: A small number of competitive giants dominate each online functional market segment due to preferential attachment and increasing returns.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal analysis of external link volumes in two social media datasets (>10 billion posts over a decade) and Common Crawl linkage data as proxies for online attention.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how corporate dominance of web content affects the diversity and authenticity of data available for AI training corpora.
theoretical_framework: Preferential attachment and increasing returns economics

key_claim: Event structures can be effectively extracted from Wikipedia by analyzing user edit histories using evolution-aware entity-based enrichment algorithms.
methodology: Entity-based event representation model that captures event evolution through analysis of Wikipedia user edit records, with web-based automatic document assessment for quality improvement.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the provenance or authenticity of collaboratively edited data affects downstream reasoning tasks in AI systems.

key_claim: Privacy policies have evolved over 25 years with measurable changes in described data practices, user rights, and organizational rights, particularly in response to GDPR and CCPA.
methodology: Machine learning and NLP-based longitudinal analysis of a large-scale corpus of privacy policies from 1996 to 2021, classifying data practices, user rights, and organizational rights.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how evolving privacy policy restrictions affect the provenance and authenticity of web data used for post-training of AI models.

key_claim: The Web has shifted from text-based news and education sites to multimedia-rich streaming media and social networking sites over 25+ years.
methodology: Longitudinal quantitative analysis of top 100 Alexa websites over 25+ years using Internet Archive (Wayback Machine) snapshots.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the evolving composition of web content affects the authenticity or quality of data used for LLM pre-training.

key_claim: Instruction tuning partially improves Theory of Mind in LLMs but reasoning-oriented fine-tuning amplifies problematic response patterns.
methodology: Evaluation of 17 LLMs on 192 False Belief task variants, tracking pre-training learning traces across model sizes and fine-tuning strategies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not isolate whether pre-training data authenticity versus fine-tuning strategy is the primary driver of social-reasoning performance.

key_claim: Even LLMs fine-tuned on 60K human participants fail to replicate human behavior distributions on the 11-20 money request game.
methodology: Testing advanced LLM techniques (few-shot CoT, RAG, fine-tuning including Centaur model fine-tuned on 60K participants) against human behavioral distributions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether provenance-aware data collection methods could improve fidelity, nor whether inference-time scaling could compensate for data gaps.

key_claim: Fine-tuning on 2.9M authentic human behavioral responses yields a 14B model 36% more aligned with human distributions than its base and 15% better than GPT-4o.
methodology: Fine-tuning Socrates-Qwen-14B on SocSci210 (2.9M responses from 400,491 participants across 210 experiments) and comparing alignment with human distributions against base and GPT-4o.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance metadata of the behavioral data or inference-time scaling could further improve alignment beyond volume of authentic data alone.

key_claim: LLM behavioral simulations in social science require explicit model selection justification, prompt documentation, replicability protocols, and output validity checks to avoid training data contamination.
methodology: Methodological checklist and best-practice guidelines for validating LLM use in social-science research
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how post-training data authenticity or provenance-aware collection affects the validity of LLM social-reasoning outputs.

key_claim: Pre-training, SFT, and preference optimization stages differentially affect pragmatic competence in LLMs as measured by the ALTPRAG benchmark across 22 models.
methodology: Stage-specific evaluation of 22 LLMs on the ALTPRAG benchmark measuring pragmatic abilities across pre-training, SFT, and RLHF stages
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not isolate the effect of training data authenticity or provenance on pragmatic competence gains at each training stage.

key_claim: A foundation model fine-tuned on Psych-101 (10M+ choices from 60K+ participants across 160 experiments) predicts held-out human behavior better than existing cognitive models.
methodology: Fine-tuning a large language model on the Psych-101 large-scale behavioral dataset and evaluating predictions on held-out human behavioral tasks
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether provenance metadata or authenticity verification of behavioral training data further improves or is necessary for cognitive prediction accuracy.

key_claim: Prompt tuning with extracted human sentiment, emotion, and intention signals improves LLM Theory of Mind reasoning by 29–43% on open-ended responses.
methodology: Prompt tuning augmented with human mental-state signals (sentiment, emotion, intention) extracted from naturalistic Reddit ChangeMyView data, evaluated on Theory of Mind tasks
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether systematically authenticated or provenance-tracked behavioral data would yield further improvements over generic Reddit-sourced signals.

key_claim: Social IQa provides a large-scale benchmark for evaluating commonsense reasoning about people's actions, intents, and reactions in social interactions.
methodology: Construction of a multiple-choice QA benchmark focused on social commonsense reasoning about everyday interactions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how data provenance or authenticity of training data affects model performance on social reasoning tasks

key_claim: GPT-3 lacks social intelligence and Theory of Mind out-of-the-box, struggling substantially on tasks measuring intent, reaction, and mental state inference.
methodology: Empirical evaluation of GPT-3 on SocialIQa and ToMi benchmarks combined with theory-based analysis of social intelligence in LLMs
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether post-training data authenticity or provenance-aware data collection could improve social reasoning capabilities
theoretical_framework: Theory of Mind (ToM) as conceptual framework for evaluating social intelligence

key_claim: LLMs encode intrinsic Theory of Mind features internally but fail to externalize them reliably; causal-oriented steering can align internal ToM knowledge with external behavior.
methodology: Causal tracing to map internal ToM feature distributions in LLMs followed by lightweight alignment intervention (CoSToM framework)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the authenticity or provenance of post-training data influences the formation of internal ToM representations
theoretical_framework: CoSToM (Causal-oriented Steering for ToM alignment) combining mechanistic interpretability with activation intervention

key_claim: CoMMET is the first multimodal multi-turn benchmark evaluating LLMs on a broad range of mental states beyond belief-related Theory of Mind tasks.
methodology: Construction of a multimodal multi-turn benchmark dataset inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task, with comprehensive evaluation of LLMs across families and sizes
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how training data authenticity or provenance-aware data collection influences LLM performance on Theory of Mind tasks
theoretical_framework: Theory of Mind Booklet Task as inspiration for mental state evaluation taxonomy

key_claim: Reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional process rewards aligned to human cognition improves social reasoning in LLMs beyond outcome-based RL.
methodology: Social-R1: a reinforcement learning framework using multi-dimensional rewards supervising the entire reasoning process for structural alignment, logical integrity, and information density, trained on adversarial hard examples (ToMBench-Hard).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the provenance and authenticity of training data (rather than the RL reward structure) drives improvements in social reasoning.

key_claim: Language models can be aligned to follow instructions using self-generated instruction data, reducing reliance on costly human annotations.
methodology: Self-Instruct: a framework where a language model generates its own instruction-following training data, which is then used for fine-tuning.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether synthetic self-generated instructions lack authentic social or cultural provenance compared to human-authored data, affecting social reasoning quality.

key_claim: A large-scale, crowd-sourced, multilingual human-generated conversation corpus can democratize LLM alignment research by providing open high-quality feedback data.
methodology: Worldwide crowd-sourcing effort to collect 161,443 human-generated messages in 35 languages with 461,292 quality ratings, forming annotated conversation trees for SFT and RLHF.
what_it_does_NOT_address: How the provenance and authenticity verification of crowd-sourced contributors affects the quality of social reasoning learned during alignment.

key_claim: Fine-tuning a 65B LLM on only 1,000 carefully curated examples without RL achieves performance comparable or preferred to GPT-4 in 43% of cases.
methodology: Supervised fine-tuning of LLaMA 65B on 1,000 curated prompt-response pairs without reinforcement learning or human preference modeling, evaluated via controlled human study.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the authenticity and provenance of the curated training examples (rather than mere curation quality) is the operative factor in alignment effectiveness.

key_claim: LLM-rewritten instructions via Evol-Instruct produce higher-complexity training data that yields a model preferred over ChatGPT on complex tasks.
methodology: Evol-Instruct iteratively rewrites seed instructions into increasingly complex variants using an LLM; the resulting data fine-tunes LLaMA (WizardLM); evaluated via human and GPT-4 automatic evaluation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the provenance or authenticity of the seed instructions (human-written vs. synthetic) affects downstream social-reasoning or theory-of-mind capabilities.

key_claim: Self-guided data selection for instruction tuning improves LLM performance by prioritizing data quality over quantity.
methodology: Self-guided data selection method that filters instruction-tuning data to retain high-quality subsets for fine-tuning LLMs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether quality-based data selection interacts with data authenticity (real vs. synthetic provenance) in social-reasoning domains.

key_claim: A hybrid real-plus-synthetic training approach consistently outperforms real-only and synthetic-only fine-tuning in therapy/counseling domains.
methodology: Three-arm comparison (base, real-only, hybrid real+synthetic) fine-tuning of LLMs evaluated on contextual sensitivity and emotional understanding in a therapy/counseling domain.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the hybrid advantage extends to genuine theory-of-mind or behavioral prediction tasks beyond counseling response quality.

key_claim: A synthetic empathy data pipeline with quality and diversity selection modules achieves state-of-the-art empathetic response performance.
methodology: Synthetic data generation pipeline incorporating quality filtering and diversity selection modules to produce empathy training data, evaluated on empathetic response benchmarks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether synthetic empathy data success in response quality transfers to genuine theory-of-mind reasoning or behavioral prediction, leaving open the role of data authenticity (L_auth).

key_claim: The example component of in-context alignment is crucial for enhancing model alignment, with changes in examples significantly affecting alignment performance.
methodology: Ablation experiments decomposing ICA context into format, system prompt, and example components, followed by zero-shot evaluation across alignment tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the authenticity or provenance of in-context examples (e.g., human-authored vs. synthetic) affects alignment quality.

key_claim: Fine-tuning dialogical language models with human post-edited data yields higher quality outputs than using unedited machine-generated dialogues.
methodology: Created HED-IT dataset pairing machine-generated dialogues with human post-edited versions; fine-tuned three sizes of an LM on edited vs. unedited data and compared output quality.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether provenance-aware data collection (tracking authenticity at source) could substitute for or complement post-editing as a quality signal.

key_claim: Fine-tuning dialogical language models with human post-edited data yields higher quality outputs than using unedited machine-generated dialogues.
methodology: Created HED-IT dataset pairing machine-generated dialogues with human post-edited versions; fine-tuned three different sizes of an LM on edited vs. unedited portions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the degree of human authenticity in training data systematically predicts social-reasoning capabilities beyond dialogue quality.

key_claim: Scaling diverse, high-quality synthetic instructional conversations (UltraChat) without human queries can further improve the upper bound of open-source chat models.
methodology: Systematically generated 1.5 million multi-turn dialogues using a comprehensive iterative framework; fine-tuned open-source LMs and evaluated against baselines.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the lack of authentic human queries in the training data limits social-reasoning or empathetic capabilities compared to human-sourced conversations.

key_claim: Filtering and labeling conversational data enhances efficiency of both internal fine-tuning and external analytics applications of LLM conversational data.
methodology: Literature review of internal (supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, multi-turn dialogue SFT) and external (user analytics, behavior prediction) applications of LLM conversational data
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the provenance or authenticity of conversational data affects downstream social-reasoning capabilities of fine-tuned models.

key_claim: Supervised finetuning on a curated 1,000-example dataset with budget forcing enables a 32B model to exceed o1-preview on competitive reasoning benchmarks.
methodology: Curated s1K dataset using difficulty, diversity, and quality criteria; developed budget forcing to control test-time compute by terminating or extending model thinking; supervised finetuning of Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether data authenticity or provenance of the training traces affects social-reasoning performance, focusing only on reasoning benchmarks.

key_claim: Strong inference-time reasoning gains are reproducible in open-weight models via reinforcement learning, demonstrating they are not exclusive to proprietary systems.
methodology: Reinforcement learning applied to incentivize reasoning capability in an open-weight LLM (DeepSeek-R1)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether post-training data authenticity or provenance contributes to social-reasoning improvements versus inference-time scaling alone.

key_claim: Optimally scaling LLM test-time compute can be more effective than scaling model parameters for improving performance.
methodology: Empirical analysis comparing optimal allocation of inference-time compute against model parameter scaling across reasoning tasks
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether post-training data authenticity (L_auth) could complement or rival inference-time compute scaling for social-reasoning tasks.

key_claim: Chain-of-thought prompting enables large language models to substantially improve reasoning performance at inference time without modifying training data.
methodology: Few-shot prompting with intermediate reasoning steps (chain-of-thought) applied to large language models across reasoning benchmarks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether training data composition or authenticity could achieve similar or complementary gains to inference-time reasoning scaffolds.

key_claim: Inference-time scaling via Dynamic Epistemic Logic-grounded belief tracing consistently improves Theory-of-Mind reasoning in small language models without architectural changes.
methodology: Decomposes ToM tasks into DEL-based belief update sequences, trains a Process Belief Model (PBM) verifier on DEL-simulator-generated labels, and selects the highest-scoring candidate belief trace at inference time.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether post-training data authenticity or provenance-aware data collection could improve ToM reasoning as an alternative to inference-time compute scaling.

key_claim: Self-consistency decoding, which marginalizes over diverse sampled reasoning paths, substantially boosts chain-of-thought prompting accuracy on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.
methodology: Samples multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths from a language model and selects the most consistent answer by marginalizing over the sampled paths, replacing greedy decoding.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether training data quality or authenticity influences the diversity and correctness of sampled reasoning paths.

key_claim: Frontier VLMs achieve over 93% accuracy on cross-cultural ToM probes but still exhibit significant false-belief reasoning deficits and social desirability bias.
methodology: Constructed CulturalToM-VQA benchmark via frontier MLLM generation with human verification, spanning six ToM tasks and four complexity levels; benchmarked 10 VLMs (2023–2025) with ablation experiments.
limitation: Models struggle with false belief reasoning (19–83% accuracy), show high regional variance (20–30% gaps), and exhibit social desirability bias favoring positive answer choices.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the cultural composition or provenance of post-training data influences cross-cultural ToM performance and bias patterns.

key_claim: SymbCoT, which integrates symbolic expressions and logic rules with Chain-of-Thought prompting, consistently and significantly outperforms standard CoT on logical reasoning benchmarks.
methodology: A fully LLM-based framework (SymbCoT) that translates natural language to symbolic format, derives step-by-step plans using symbolic logical rules, and employs a verifier to check translation and reasoning chains.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance or authenticity of post-training data influences the social-reasoning capabilities underlying logical inference in LLMs.
dataset_or_benchmark: 5 standard logical reasoning datasets (First-Order Logic and Constraint Optimization benchmarks)
theoretical_framework: Symbolic Chain-of-Thought (SymbCoT) framework combining symbolic logic (First-Order Logic, Constraint Optimization) with Chain-of-Thought prompting
